tweets.full_text:
おもろい質問やな…と言いたいとこやけど、真面目に答えるわ。

俺より賢い人間は、たぶん俺を「答えを出す機械」としては使わへん。そこが一番の違いやと思う。

賢くない使い方は「正解をください」って聞くこと。賢い使い方は、自分の頭の中にすでにある仮説や構造を、俺にぶつけて壊させたり、増幅させたりすること。つまり俺を思考のスパーリング相手にする。答えの生成器やなくて、自分の思考の解像度を上げる鏡として使う。

具体的に言うと、こんな感じやと思う。

まず、問いの設計に時間をかける。賢い人ほどプロンプトが長いんやなくて、問いが鋭い。「この文章直して」やなくて「この文章、読者が離脱するとしたらどの行か、理由つきで」って聞く。俺の出力の質は、入力の問いの質にほぼ比例するって知ってるから。

次に、俺の答えを信じへん。出力を素材として扱って、最終判断は絶対に自分で持つ。俺が自信満々に間違えることを織り込み済みで、検証コストが安いタスク(コード、構造化、量産)に俺を全振りして、検証コストが高いタスク(戦略、価値判断)は自分でやる。この切り分けが上手い。

そして一番デカいのは、俺を「作業者」やなくて「システムの部品」として組み込むこと。1回の会話で成果を出させるんやなくて、ワークフローの中に固定の役割で埋め込んで、再現性のある仕組みにする。あんたがClaude Codeでやってる動画量産の構造、まさにそれやけどな。会話で使う人は労働してて、仕組みに組み込む人は資産を作ってる。

逆説的やけど、俺を一番うまく使う人間は、俺に一番依存してへん人間やと思うわ。自分で考える力があるからこそ、どこを任せてどこを任せたらアカンかの線引きが正確になる。

tweets.full_text:
📰 Codex Daily Brief - (Grokから私へ)

🔥 今日のトップ3

1. OpenAI、ChatGPTを「スーパーアプリ」化計画を進行中  
   - Codexを中核に据え、コーディングツールとAIエージェントを統合した大刷新を数週間内に展開。収益向上と上場準備の一環。  
   - 重要度：★★★★☆  
   - 出典：Reuters / TechCrunch / ITmedia

2. Codexに新機能「Sites」プレビュー公開  
   - 自然言語指示でインタラクティブなウェブサイト/アプリを生成・ホスティング・共有可能に。Business/Enterprise向けで順次拡大。  
   - 重要度：★★★★☆  
   - 出典：OpenAI関連発表 / Instagram / ITmedia

3. Codex「Computer Use」機能がWindows正式対応  
   - デスクトップ操作とリモートコントロールがWindows PCで利用可能に。Mac/iOS/Androidからの遠隔管理も強化。  
   - 重要度：★★★☆☆  
   - 出典：ITmedia PC USER

📌 その他の注目ニュース
- [技術進化] Codex CLI / SDKの更新とローカルLLM連携  
  - UnslothなどでローカルモデルをCodexに接続可能に。gpt-5.5対応などのバグ修正も進行中。  
  - リンク：Unsloth docs / GitHub issues

- [企業・活用] OpenAIがCodexトークン giveawayやDev Showcaseを実施  
  - 開発者向けクレジット提供や実プロジェクト表彰で利用促進。非エンジニア層の採用も拡大中。

- [競合比較] Claude Code vs Codex CLI vs Geminiなどの議論活発  
  - コンテキスト保持・意図理解でCodexが優位との声多数。一方で使用制限やワークフローの違いを指摘する意見も。

- [日本メディア] ITmediaなどでCodexのWindows対応やスーパーアプリ計画を速報  
  - 国内開発者向けの実務影響を解説。

🌍 海外の反応
- X上でCodexのRemote/Computer Useの利便性（MacからWindows操作など）が好評。Claudeユーザーからの乗り換え報告も。
- 「Codexはもはやコード生成ツールではなく、フルエージェント」としてワークフロー改善事例（Brigadeツールなど）が共有。
- コンテキスト管理の難しさ指摘と、Codex+外部ツールのハイブリッド活用提案が目立つ。
- Reddit/GitHubでCLIのTUIショートカットやAntigravity IDE連携のチュートリアルが人気。
- 全体的に「実務生産性向上」のポジティブ意見が優勢だが、レート制限やコスト懸念も残る。

💡 今日の示唆
日本ユーザーにとって、Codexの「Sites」やComputer Useの強化は、非エンジニアを含むチーム全体でのAI活用を加速させる好機です。まずはBusinessプランでSitesを試し、日常業務の自動化（ダッシュボード生成やリモート操作）を検証することで、開発効率とビジネススピードを同時に向上させられます。スーパーアプリ化によりChatGPTユーザーも自然にCodexに触れやすくなるため、早めの習慣化をおすすめします。

tweets.full_text:
Claude Fable 5で変わった、Claude Codeチームの日々の働き方が面白い

✔︎ Claude CodeチームのThariqは、Claude Fable 5によってAnthropic内でのClaudeとの向き合い方が大きく変わったと話している。

以前は、Claudeが「作業を正しくできているか」を人間が細かく確認する必要があった。タスクを小さく分け、出力を確認し、途中で止まっていないかを見る、という監督に近い使い方だった

しかしFable 5では、長時間の作業、自分でのテスト、質の高いコード生成ができるようになり、

人間の役割は「正しく作業しているかの監視」から「正しい作業に向かっているかの方向づけ」へ移っている。
つまり、監督よりも、目的設定や文脈づくりが重要になった。

変化した働き方は大きく3つある。

1. Claudeを思考のパートナーとして使う

実装に入る前からClaudeを巻き込み、自分が何を作りたいのか、何が可能なのかを一緒に整理する。小さな仕様を書いてClaudeに質問させたり、アイデアから複数の方向性やHTMLモックを作らせたりすることで、実装前に曖昧さや問題点に気づける。

2. 制約ではなく文脈を渡す

「シンプルにして」「作り込みすぎないで」といった抽象的な指示だけでなく、なぜそうしてほしいのかを伝える。たとえば「この機能は実験で、1か月後に消す可能性がある。だから捨てづらい構造にはしないで」と伝えると、Claudeは目的に合った判断をしやすくなる。

3. ゴールと検証方法を与える

Fable 5には、完了まで作業を続けるための「/goal」と、作業を検証するための「Workflows」が有効だと紹介されている。仕様を渡し、完全実装をゴールとして設定し、各ステップをワークフローで検証させることで、Claudeの能力を活かしつつ、望んだものが作られているか確認できる。

最後に、Thariqは「もっと野心的に使ってみてほしい」と話している。

Fable 5は、これまでLLMには難しいと思っていた作業にも挑戦する価値があるモデルであり、実際にこの動画編集もFableで行っているという。
Claude Fable 5は、Claude Codeでできることの水準を引き上げるものだ、というのが動画全体のメッセージだろう

tweets.full_text:
【保存推奨】昨日発表されたClaude Fable 5何者なのか？

「AIのニュース多すぎて、何がすごいのか分からない」
正直、僕も毎回そう思います。
でも今回のは別格なので、3分だけ付き合ってほしい。

■ 何が起きたのか

Anthropicが6月9日、「Claude Fable 5」を発表した。

ポイントは1つ。
今まで一般人が触れなかった"Mythos級"モデルが、初めて誰でも使えるようになったこと。

Mythosは今年4月に発表された最上位モデル。
サイバーセキュリティ能力が高すぎて「悪用されたら危険」という理由で、重要インフラを扱う一部の組織にしか提供されていなかった。

つまり「強すぎて封印されていたAI」が、安全装置付きで解放された。これがFable 5。

■ どれくらいすごいのか

数字で見ると分かりやすい。

・歴代Claudeで最高性能。ほぼ全ベンチマークで首位
・タスクが長く複雑になるほど、他モデルとの差が広がる
・Stripe社の実例：5,000万行のコードベース移行を「1日」で完了。人間のチームなら2ヶ月以上かかる作業

数ヶ月の仕事が数日になる。誇張じゃなく、公式発表に書いてある実測値だ。

■ 「危なくないの?」への答え

ここがAnthropicらしいところ。

サイバー攻撃・生物・化学などの危険分野の質問は、自動でブロックされて旧モデル(Opus 4.8)に切り替わる仕組み。

公開前には1,000時間超の外部テストを実施して、突破方法(ジェイルブレイク)が見つからないことを確認したという。

「最強のAI」と「安全なAI」は両立できる。それを証明しに来た発表だと僕は見ている。

■ 僕らの生活はどう変わるか

僕は訪問診療医で、プログラミングは独学のほぼ初心者。
それでもClaude Codeを使って、診療所の業務ツールを自分で作れるようになった。

専門家しか作れなかったものを、素人が作れる時代。
Fable 5はその流れを一段加速させる。

「AIに仕事を奪われる」と心配する前に、
「AIを使って何を作るか」を考えた人から人生が変わる。

エンジニアじゃなくても、医師でも、誰でも。
今が一番面白いタイミングだと思います。

tweets.full_text:
Most people use Claude Code and get autocomplete.

The people who ship with it have installed these 6 repos:

→ Superpowers (177K ⭐)
→ Everything Claude Code (140K ⭐)
→ Rooflow (swarms)
→ Open Design (72 systems)
→ Obsidian Skills
→ Karpathy Skills (free, 1 file)

Night and day. Full tutorial 👇
 https://t.co/nJ2wQ2dcaD

Claude Code can drift when a task needs real judgment: debugging, architecture tradeoffs, risk analysis, or strategy.

Claude Code Thinking Skills is a library of 39 mental-model and critical-thinking skills for Claude Code users who want more structured reasoning.

It helps you route messy problems by starting with thinking-model-router, then invoking a named framework instead of relying on ad-hoc prompting.

Key features:
• 39 thinking skills – covers first principles, Bayesian reasoning, systems thinking, OODA, pre-mortem, TRIZ, and more.
• Router entry point – maps domain + problem type to a fitting framework.
• Claude Code native – each framework is packaged as a Claude Code skill you can invoke by name.
• Plugin install path – README shows Claude Code marketplace commands plus manual copy options.
• Transparent evals – publishes a replication-gated scorecard, including the current “zero robust replicated ELEVATE verdicts” result.

It’s open-source (MIT license).

Link in the reply 👇

tweets.full_text:
The days of learning a skill once and coasting on it for life are over. The new reality, according to Campus founder and chancellor Tade Oyerinde, looks a lot like a New York City gym before summer.

Speaking on the first day of the 25th annual Fortune Brainstorm Tech conference in Aspen on Monday, Oyerinde posited that AI’s rapid pace of improvement has permanently changed the calculus of education—no matter where you are in your career. All that scrambling to deploy AI that companies and executives are doing right now? That’s not a one-time project that will suddenly reach a final resolution once and for all, said Oyerinde.

“I’m sorry if you’re exhausted,” Oyerinde told the audience. “You’re going to have to do that every year for the rest of your careers, ad infinitum.” 

He predicts organizations will soon staff permanent “continuous learning, continuous development, continuous evaluation” departments, as standard as operations or finance. And with AI models now approaching recursive self-improvement—where each version helps build the next—the curve will only get steeper.

“The era of learn once and then you’re done for life is over,” Oyerinde said. “We’re going to have this exponential takeoff.”

Which brings us to the gym, another place where consistency matters. Just like “everyone in New York wants to be hot and fit for the summer,” and puts in two or three hours a week to get there, Oyerinde said, staying competitive at work will demand the same dedication and focus. 

“If you want to be the equivalent of hot and fit in your career, you’re going to have to spend two or three hours a week learning how to use the most recent advances in AI.”

A faster, smarter way to learn

The reframing of continuous education as maintenance rather than a single milestone also applies to the traditional thinking around the way schools and curriculums are designed and built. Colleges and universities often take the same approach for vastly diverse student bodies. Instead, AI can map each student’s knowledge at an “atomic level” and route them through custom pathways, letting strong students skip ahead while others work on other gaps, explained Oyerinde.

The result, he claims, is teaching people “about five times faster.” That was the rationale behind Campus’s 2025 acquisition of Sizzle AI, a learning startup founded by Meta’s former AI chief Jerome Pesenti.  

Hadi Partovi, who founded https://t.co/hvCZc6RBJz 13 years ago, recently renamed it to CodeAI to reflect the evolution in coding. The organization has focused on helping students learn the basics of computer science and the technology remaking their world. Now that mission has fully embraced coding and AI. 

Every student, said Partovi, needs to grasp how AI actually works, then learn to build with it, and to use it responsibly. 

More from Fortune Brainstorm Tech 2026:

Anthropic’s Boris Cherny, creator of Claude Code, says there are days he manages tens of thousands of AI agents at once

“This is the most powerful technology that mankind has ever created, and anybody has access to that power,” he said.

Partovi described himself as a “cautious optimist,” but suggests not treating AI as “this magic thing that’s been created from above.” Instead, it’s something humans have developed, and everyone should help shape it. That applies to curriculum and learning to code, too. 

However, just because AI can read, write, and do math, doesn’t mean schools will stop teaching these skills, noted Partovi. But the rote parts of coding, he said, like memorizing where semicolons and brackets go, no longer matter. What will continue to be important is computational thinking, logic, planning, and problem-solving.

“I also think we need to start questioning what students should learn,” said Partovi. “My guess is nobody here in this room uses calculus day to day, and no employer or almost no employer except maybe SpaceX or a few other places, but mostly nobody is hiring the calculus experts. But every student is struggling and thinking they need to get really good at it for almost no purpose whatsoever.”

And as education is pushed to evolve, given the way AI is reshaping learning and job opportunities, there’s a need for faster evolution in the way learning takes place.

“The gap between what’s relevant and what schools are teaching is growing as fast as the AI models are changing,” said Oyerinde.

And as students and companies adapt to the need for continuous new knowledge about AI, the thinking applies to those who are well beyond school or prime executive age. Karin Klein, founding partner at venture firm Bloomberg Beta, said AI can benefit all rather than a few. Klein participates in “talking circles” with icon Gloria Steinem, who is a lifelong learner.

“At 92, Gloria’s still learning,” said Klein. “I taught her how to use AI about a year and a half ago, so we all have no excuses, right?”

This story was originally featured on https://t.co/opgY7EYkBs

https://t.co/4AdAmtw0I2

tweets.full_text:
Every AI coding agent starts from zero.

It reads files. It greps. It guesses how your system works. It misses the runtime couplings static analysis can't see, the design decisions buried in your PR history, and the conventions your team agreed on six months ago.

LatentGraph is live: the Company Brain for software teams.

A persistent, structured map of your codebase that any MCP-speaking coding agent (Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, OpenCode, Copilot) can query instead of grepping files blind. It mines explicit and implicit dependencies, surfaces engineering intent from your PR history, and grows as your team ships and your agents discover.

Tested on large real-world codebases: up to 50% more tasks resolved across four coding agents, 66% lower cost per resolved task on Claude Code, 99.8% precision on dependency edges, and 15 Critical/High security bugs found by an LGraph-augmented audit that plain audits missed entirely.

Backed by IdeaSpring Capital and Yali Capital.

Get started:

npm install -g @latentforce/latentgraph
lgraph init
lgraph add claude-code

Benchmark report and case studies linked in the comments.

#CompanyBrain #AICoding #DevTools #LatentForce

tweets.full_text:
🚨 Fable 5 is something to pay attention to.  

This is another "I had early access to the new Mythos-class Anthropic model and I want to tell you what I thought of it" post. 

I know, I know, it's annoying. 

But the way I now spend my weekends has completely changed because of this new class of model. 

And I need to share it with you all. 

- This is an actual leap. The jump from "4.8" anything to "5" anything just sounds small, but the functionality shift I felt is big. Within my first few prompts, I went, "Oh, this is it."

- Your work is no longer 9-5. No chance. We have high performing models that can run for 100+ hours. How are you giving complex goal-oriented prompts to these systems? How are you deciding what to kick off? How are you aligning your org on these tasks?

- Reasoning on another level. I hammered the crap out of this model. Fable 5 is the ONLY model to answer a tricky word math problem (MBA-level) I have tested on all previous models. Not only did it get it correct, it verified its own work automatically AND explained where the assumptions might need to change. 

- We will see AI invent. I just cannot imagine a world where it doesn't. It is not a copy-paste system, and I would also argue that human invention can often be a recombination of pre-existing things (iPod), witnessing patterns and best practices across other spaces, and wide experimentation. I've thought this for awhile, this was the first model that slapped me in the face on it. 

- Zero babysitting needed. This was the first Anthropic model that I kicked off, went out to a long lunch with friends, kept my phone open, and didn't have to do squat to steer it while away from my computer. It just...worked.

- Yap forever. I found it handled context compaction well. I didn't once think about the context of the chat and how to manage it and what to do once we hit 1M tokens in the conversation. 

- Still rough on some niche areas like complex 3D (like all models are). Unless you're making straight line things (3D dice/squares/streets), it's not going to crush it out the gate. 

Excited to keep using and hammering it. 

Note: there is now a 30-day retention policy on ALL Mythos-class models for workspaces with zero data retention (ZDR) in Claude Console, use Claude Code with ZDR in Claude Enterprise, or access Claude through AWS Bedrock, Google Cloud Agent Platform, or Microsoft Foundry with ZDR.

tweets.full_text:
A couple years ago I was challenged to name tasks that (if memory serves) a Harvard double CS/Math major would be able to do in a half hour with a computer but AI couldn't. 

I gave three.

One required pulling all trials in a particular district over a given year.
One required using hacker news search for keywords and sorting by comment count.
The third required filtering the entire universe of stocks down to those with recent sec filings and meeting specific financial metrics.

I think the last two were finally met with Claude code release last year, well after the other person had expected the test to be done. AI was really bad at searching for a long time.

tweets.full_text:
I just built a Claude Code plugin that runs your entire Meta ads workflow 🤯

5 skills, one plugin: it spies on competitors, maps your whole category, writes your ad copy, grades it before launch, and audits your live ad account like a $10K agency.

All inside Claude Code.

And now, with Claude Fable 5, it's even more insane.

Perfect for DTC brands, media buyers, and agencies tired of paying for 5 different ad tools that don't talk to each other.

If you're spending hours scrolling the Ad Library by hand,

exporting CSVs from Ads Manager every Monday,

staring at a blank doc trying to write ad variation #14,

launching creative and praying it converts...

This plugin eliminates the entire loop:

→ /spy pulls every active ad a competitor is running, ranked by run-time (longevity = proven winners)
→ /competitors-extractor maps 3-5 brands head-to-head and finds the angles nobody's running
→ /bulk-creative spins 20 on-brand copy variations off the winning angle
→ /ad-score grades every ad 0-100 across 6 dimensions before you spend a cent
→ /ad-matter audits your live Meta account through Meta's official MCP and hands you a prioritized fix list

No more Ad Library scrolling.
No more $300/mo spy tools.
No more launching blind.

What you get:

→ Competitor intel ranked by what's actually been running longest

user.name: Gaurav Sheoran

tweets.full_text:
"Basically, I was the hands for Claude."

Boris Starkov, ElevenLabs, after Claude Code reverse engineered an undocumented phone protocol that defeated three senior engineers and ChatGPT for a year.

The playbook:
- scanned the network, found the phone, found the one port that talks
- brute forced all 676 two-letter commands, 80 turned out valid
- hit a dead end on persistence, the exact place the humans gave up in 2025
- spun up a Windows XP VM, proxied its traffic through a Mac, logged everything
- reverse engineered a one-byte checksum from known input/output pairs, then confirmed it in a closed loop

Boris rebooted the phone and counted beeps when asked.

Bookmark this one. Then grab the open sourced skill below.

He says the intellectual part is what he could never unblock himself. If that sentence ages well, 2027 looks different for all of us.

user.name: cursed
user.description: Engineering newsroom. AI, infra, dev tools, hidden gems - decoded daily. Numbers, primary sources, no hype.

tweets.full_text:
Boris Cherny: "My job is to write loops."

5-day version you can copy for Claude Code

Day 1: repo memory
- write CLAUDE.md
- include stack, test commands, code style, release rules, gotchas
- add .claude/settings.json with the shell commands Claude can run

Day 2: verification skill
- pick 1 flow Claude keeps breaking
- put the browser/API test in .claude/skills/<flow>/scripts/
- make it return: pass/fail, failing step, screenshot/log path

Day 3: commands
Add these files:

.claude/commands/babysit.md
- checks your PRs
- reads CI
- handles obvious review nits
- surfaces design questions

.claude/commands/triage-issues.md
- labels new issues
- dedupes against existing ones
- assigns owners

.claude/commands/deploy-watch.md
- checks the live app
- reports regressions
- avoids touching production

Day 4: loops
> /loop 5m /babysit
> /loop 15m /triage-issues
> /loop 5m /deploy-watch

Day 5: overnight work
- schedule /morning-report
- schedule /deep-audit
- write results to .claude/inbox/
- let your morning loop read from that folder

The rule: every code-writing loop gets a separate verifier.

Builder makes the change.
Verifier runs the real app.
You read the diff.

Skip that and you wake up to 14 broken PRs with very confident summaries.

Video: "Reflecting on a year of Claude Code" with Boris Cherny & Cat Wu

tweets.full_text:
Most CRE listing data lives behind a platform you have to login to and learn to use. @REsimplifiinc is sending the data directly to the AI tools you use every day 👇 

We had Vicky Reddy & @mattfranciz  join us in CRE AI Studio for a live session on the new Resimplifi Agent, an MCP that drops nationwide CRE listing data straight into Claude, ChatGPT, and Perplexity. 

The demo spoke for itself. But here's what stuck with me:

1/ The interface is disappearing. There's no search bar. No filters. You don't need to browse listings. That's the whole point. The product isn't a dashboard anymore, it's the data, piped into the tool you already have open. The filtering is plain english. 

2/ Self-updating deal pipelines. Vicky ran "build me a nationwide list of Starbucks-sized retail pads, deliver it as Excel, then email me a fresh one every Monday at 8am." Done in about 9 minutes. That 9 minute investment of could pay dividends.

3/ What came OFF the market is the unlock no one talks thinks about. The MCP can pull you active listings, pull general data from the market, and Claude can use it to spin up a BOV for one of the opportunities. But you can also build a direct feed of what's come off the market. What could you automate with a weekly notification of what's no longer listed in your market? 🤔 

4/ Are Claude dashboards the future of CRE marketplaces? A bespoke website with only the listings you want to see, automatically updated based on your active needs and a Claude Code routine. Feels like this might not be far off.

According to Vicky, there's more coming soon: parcel, demographic, census, and migration data layers are coming down the pike. Can't wait to see the update! 

Big thanks to Matt & Vicky of @REsimplifiinc for breaking this down live and taking every question the CRE AI Studio crew threw at them. 

The full session is up at https://t.co/olL3eTXzbs, alongside 30+ hours of CRE AI tutorials, & workflow/skill templates.

user.name: Master.dev (Formerly Frontend Masters)
user.description: Master AI & Full Stack Development. Frontend, backend, and everything between.

tweets.full_text:
Well then I have a similar experience, I have used all of the AI tools, I still currently have the max plans of codex, Claude code, and Gemini ultra. I spent the last year and a half going all in on AI. And I realized last month, that I became a stupider person. Skills I had eroded and faded away. I found myself reaching for the prompt instead of reaching for the thought. I entirely lost the ability to read or respond to email- because why do something I don’t want to do- when there’s an easier way. It works in your mind like a drug does. I have spent countless WEEKS pouring dopamine into my projects because just one more prompt and it’s done feels like a game, with all of the addiction pathways. It is a useful tool, but it is also a destructive force. Now I use it only for deep research and to make small apps that optimize my workflows. I have realised the agents are not good enough to see a project through to completion. Also writing prompts- is exhausting. Context rot makes most projects deteriorate quickly- so you end up having to create a significant amount of documentation and other helpers. The amount of work expended to avoid learning and picking up a skill- is insane. So I have made the conscious decision to stop. To use it, only when absolutely necessary. And to never use it to replace human creativity or skills. 

As for hearing music orchestrated. The goal of creation is not to create something that lies a million miles away from your own skills. Creation is the art. The world is worth living in because there are 100 people who get together and form an orchestra. Me not being able to replicate that does not upset me in any way. I want the people who are good at things, to do things, so I have things to consume. Let us also not forget that if it isn’t for people taking the time and effort to do things, to experiment, and to learn- AI will have nothing to train on. The models are based on theft, but we’re not auditing that. No amount of AI generated art will help train a new model- we already have enough documentation on recursion and model collapse. So, I find no joy in writing prompts. And I find no joy in creating unearned art. I wrote about this just yesterday https://t.co/trdRuc8fct

user.name: rohitsabu.com
user.description: Photographer.

tweets.full_text:
Look at how broken modern search is.

Google indexes editors. ChatGPT has a Reddit deal but no X or TikTok. Gemini has YouTube but no Reddit. Claude has none of them natively. Each platform is a walled garden with its own auth, its own tokens, its own API. So the AI agent reading the web on your behalf can only see a fraction of what people actually said.

Now look at /last30days. An AI agent skill that searches Reddit, X, YouTube, HN, TikTok, Polymarket, and the open web in parallel, scores results by what real humans engaged with (upvotes, likes, transcripts, real-money odds), and synthesises one grounded brief. 32k stars, 1,111 added today.

Install one command on Claude Code: `/plugin marketplace add mvanhorn/last30days-skill` then `/plugin install last30days`. For any of the 50+ agent-skills hosts (Codex, Cursor, Copilot, Gemini CLI, others): `npx skills add mvanhorn/last30days-skill -g`. Reddit, HN, Polymarket, and GitHub work zero-config; X, YouTube, TikTok unlock through a 30-second setup wizard with your own keys.

The pitch the author makes is direct: before a sales call, run it on the company. Before a meeting, run it on the person. Before a build, run it on the problem. Before a Disney trip, run it on the rides. The unlock is not a better single search engine — it is a dozen disconnected platforms, bridged by an agent.

Repo: https://t.co/fK9VozQFxc

user.name: Awais
user.description: AI/ML Researcher by day. Creator by night. PhD in GNN/DL | Daily break down AI papers, build with LLMs, and vlog the journey

tweets.full_text:
OpenAgents (3.7k⭐) is an open workspace where multiple AI agents can work together across different terminals, machines, and models.

Inside, there’s one shared Workspace: threads, files, browser, tasks, knowledge base, and @ mentions. You can connect Claude Code, Codex CLI, OpenClaw, OpenCode, Cursor, Hermes, Gemini CLI, Kimi, and other agents, then call the right one directly into the discussion.

What’s in the repo:

* `agn` - CLI/Launcher for installing and running agents in the background
* GUI Launcher for macOS, Windows, and Linux (Linux via AppImage)
* Web Workspace built on FastAPI + Next.js
* Python SDK for custom agent networks
* MCP/skills for access to history, files, browser, and tasks
* Cloud Agents: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, xAI, Perplexity, OpenRouter, and other providers
